title: read_bits unsafe pointer dereference reads past buffer boundary
description: BitPacker::read_bits constructs a subslice via data[word_idx * 8..] and casts as_ptr() to *const u64, always reading 8 bytes regardless of remaining slice length. An attacker who controls on-disk compression metadata (dict_count) can trigger OOB read past the 4096-byte page boundary, leaking adjacent memory or causing segfault.
reasoning: Line 73: `&*(data[word_idx * 8..].as_ptr() as *const u64)`. The slice bounds-check is bypassed by the raw pointer dereference. Triggered from dict.rs:102 where src[packed_start..] with dict_count=511 creates a 4-byte subslice, but bit_width=41 requires ~164 bytes. First fast-path read at word_idx=0 reads 8 bytes from page offset 4092, with bytes 4096-4099 past the page allocation.
suggested_fix: Replace unsafe pointer cast with safe slice read: use `u64::from_le_bytes(data[word_idx * 8..word_idx * 8 + 8].try_into()?)` and add a length check before taking the fast path.

title: write_bits unsafe pointer dereference writes 8 bytes past buffer boundary
description: BitPacker::write_bits uses the same unsafe pointer cast for writes. Writing 8 bytes via *mut u64 past the valid slice corrupts heap-adjacent memory. Attacker-controlled bit_width or undersized dst buffer triggers heap buffer overflow.
reasoning: Line 43: `&mut *(data[word_idx * 8..].as_mut_ptr() as *mut u64)` writes 8 bytes irrespective of remaining slice length. Same logic flaw as read_bits.
suggested_fix: Replace with safe write: use byte-level bit manipulation via safe indexing into data[], or add explicit bounds check: if word_idx * 8 + 8 > data.len() { fall through to byte-by-byte path }.

title: Unvalidated dict_count from untrusted page enables OOB read via BitPacker
description: DictCompression::decompress_from_page reads dict_count from the first 4 bytes of the compressed page with no validation. An attacker can set dict_count up to 511, making packed_start = 4092, producing a 4-byte subslice. BitPacker::unpack_32 with the computed bit_width (~41) needs ~164 bytes, causing OOB read through the unsafe pointer in read_bits.
reasoning: Line 86-88 reads dict_count from src[..4] unchecked. Line 99 computes bit_width from dict_count. Line 102 passes src[packed_start..] to unpack_32. For dict_count >= 511, packed_start + 8 >= 4100, exceeding PAGE_SIZE=4096. The unsafe dereference in BitPacker::read_bits then reads past allocation boundary.
suggested_fix: Validate dict_count <= 32 (max values per block) and verify packed_start + needed_bytes <= src.len() before calling unpack_32. Return error on invalid values.

title: Integer underflow in read_string offset computation causes crash/DoS
description: OverflowFile::read_string takes offset: u16 (max 65535) but page usable_size is 4092. When offset > 4092, `usable_size - current_offset` produces a usize underflow (panic in debug, wrapping in release). Both modes result in a panic on the subsequent slice access at line 44. Attacker-controlled overflow page metadata crashes the database.
reasoning: Line 43: usable_size (4092) - current_offset (u16, up to 65535). In debug mode, underflow panics immediately. In release mode, the wrapped value produces a massive to_read, then `page_data[current_offset..current_offset + to_read]` panics because current_offset > 4096.
suggested_fix: Add validation at function entry: if offset as usize >= usable_size return an error. Also validate len to prevent OOM via String::with_capacity(large_len).

title: Integer overflow in node_id multiplication bypasses bounds checks
description: CSRIndex::for_each_neighbor computes byte offset via node_id * 8. When node_id > u64::MAX / 8, the multiplication wraps in release mode, producing an incorrect page index that can pass the page-count bounds check and read from wrong index data.
reasoning: Line 30-31: `(node_id * 8) / PAGE_SIZE` with no checked/saturating arithmetic. node_id originates from database row values via recursive_join or shortest_path operators. Although auto-increment IDs are bounded, crafted edge rows could inject large IDs into the traversal queue.
suggested_fix: Use checked_mul: `let byte_offset = node_id.checked_mul(8).ok_or(LightningError::Internal(...))?` or validate node_id against known max_node_id before computation.
